description_long: This is a traditional Japanese wooden building with a prominent, steeply pitched tiled roof, characteristic of Edo-period architecture. The roof is covered in dark blue-gray tiles with visible ridges and a row of decorative end caps along the eaves. The facade features a series of sliding shoji doors with white paper panels framed in dark wood, providing both access and natural light. The structure sits on a solid wooden base, with the wood grain clearly visible, giving the building a sturdy and historic appearance. The overall design is minimalist, with little exterior ornamentation aside from the roof detailing, emphasizing function and harmony with the environment. This type of building is often found as a historical attraction in Japanese towns, serving as a preserved residence, merchant house, or cultural site.
description: A traditional Japanese wooden building with a steep tiled roof and sliding shoji doors, featuring minimal ornamentation and a sturdy wooden base.
description_view_0: The gabled end of the building is visible, showing the steeply pitched tiled roof and plain wooden wall beneath.
description_view_1: The main facade displays multiple sliding shoji doors framed in dark wood beneath the wide tiled roof.
description_view_2: The opposite gabled end mirrors the first, with the same steep roof and unadorned wooden wall.
description_view_3: Another view of the main facade, similar to view 1, showing the row of shoji doors and the expansive tiled roof.
